5 min read
Just now

--

n-Aware RAG. Here’s What Actually Broke in Production.
The Setup That Bit Me
Six months ago, I shipped a RAG pipeline to production. It worked beautifully in testing — 92% accuracy on our eval set, sub-200ms latency,
clean logs. Two weeks later, the on-call rotation woke me at 3 AM. The pipeline was returning garbage for 40
% of queries. The eval set hadn’t changed. The model hadn’t changed. What changed was the distribution of user questions.
Users started asking follow-ups. “What about X?” “And Y?” The retriever, built for standalone questions, couldn’t handle conversational context. It retrieved documents for “Y” in isolation, missing that “Y” only made sense after “X.” The fix wasn’t a better model. It was a conversation-aware retriever that rewrites follow-ups into standalone queries before searching.
This pattern — context loss between conversation turns — is the single most common production RAG failure I’ve seen across six different deployments. It’s not a model quality issue. It’s a plumbing issue. And it’s completely solvable with about fifty lines of code.
What I Actually Built to Fix It
The pattern is straightforward: intercept the user’s message, check if it’s a follow-up, rewrite it with full context, then pass the rewritten query to your existing retriever. Here’s the component that does the heavy lifting:
class ConversationAwareRetriever:
def __init__(self, base_retriever, llm, max_history: int = 6):
self.base_retriever = base_retriever
self.llm = llm
self.max_history = max_history def _rewrite_query(self, query: str, history: list[dict]) -> str:
if not history:
return query conversation = "\n".join(
f"{msg['role']}: {msg['content']}"
for msg in history[-self.max_history:]
) prompt = f"""Rewrite the user's follow-up question as a standalone query.
Include all context needed to answer it without seeing the conversation.Conversation:
{conversation}Follow-up: {query}Standalone query:""" response = self.llm.invoke(prompt)
return response.content.strip() def retrieve(self, query: str, history: list[dict] = None) -> list[Document]:
standalone = self._rewrite_query(query, history or [])
return self.base_retriever.get_relevant_documents(standalone)This wrapper sits in front of any retriever — vector store, BM25, hybrid. It adds ~150ms latency for the rewrite call but eliminates the 40% failure rate on follow-ups. The trade-off is worth it every time.
Let me walk through a concrete example. User asks: “How do I configure the retry policy?” The retriever finds the config docs. Next turn: “What about the timeout setting?” Without context, the retriever searches for “timeout setting” and returns generic HTTP timeout docs. With the rewriter, the query becomes “How do I configure the retry policy timeout setting?” — and the retriever finds the exact subsection about retry timeouts.
The best production AI patterns aren’t clever model tricks. They’re boring plumbing that makes the model’s job easier.
The Pattern I Reuse Now
After fixing the follow-up problem, I started seeing the same shape everywhere. Context loss is the silent killer of production RAG. It shows up in three forms:

• Conversational drift: Follow-ups lose context (what I just showed you)

• Session drift: Long conversations exceed the retriever’s context window

• Implicit reference: “That thing we discussed” with no explicit anchor
The solution pattern is always the same: make context explicit before retrieval. Here are the three variants I now ship by default:
1. Conversation Rewriter (for follow-ups)
Shown above. Rewrites each user turn into a standalone query using recent history. Cost: one LLM call per turn. Benefit: retriever sees complete intent.
The key design decision is how much history to include. I default to six messages (three user-assistant pairs). That covers most follow-up chains without bloating the prompt. For domains with longer context dependencies — legal review, code debugging — I bump it to ten. The rewrite prompt itself is deliberately minimal: just the conversation and a clear instruction. No few-shot examples needed; the task is simple enough that zero-shot works reliably with any decent instruction-following model.
2. Rolling Summary (for long sessions)
When history exceeds ~4,000 tokens, summarize the earliest N messages into a single “context note” and prepend it to the rewrite prompt. The retriever gets compressed but complete context.
Implementation detail: I keep a running summary that gets updated every N turns. When the conversation hits the token threshold, I ask the LLM to condense the oldest half into a paragraph, prepend it to the remaining history, and continue. The rewrite prompt then sees: [summary] + [recent messages]. This way the retriever never loses the thread, even in hour-long debugging sessions.
There’s a subtle trap here: if you summarize too aggressively, you lose the specific details the retriever needs. I’ve found that keeping the last 2,000 tokens verbatim and only summarizing the older half strikes the right balance. The summary captures “we discussed retry policies and circuit breakers” while the recent messages preserve “the timeout is 30 seconds and max retries is 3.”
3. Entity Anchor (for implicit references)
Track named entities (products, tickets, people) mentioned in conversation. When the user says “that issue,” inject the most recent entity of type “issue” into the rewritten query. Simple heuristic, massive precision gain.
I implement this with a lightweight entity extractor that runs on each assistant response. It pulls out proper nouns tagged by type: PRODUCT, TICKET, PERSON, FILE, CONFIG_KEY. The entity store is just a deque with a TTL of twenty turns. When rewriting, if the query contains demonstratives (“that,” “this,” “those”) without a clear noun, I prepend the most recent matching entity. “What about the timeout?” becomes “What about the retry policy timeout setting?” if “retry policy” was the last PRODUCT entity mentioned.
The entity extractor doesn’t need a fancy NER model. A simple prompt with a few examples works well enough: “Extract named entities from this response. Return JSON with type and value.” The types are domain-specific — for a dev-tools RAG, I use PRODUCT, CONFIG_KEY, ERROR_CODE, FILE_PATH. For a support bot, it’s TICKET_ID, PRODUCT, ACCOUNT_ID. The key is keeping the type taxonomy small and stable.
All three are pre-retrieval transformations. They don’t touch your embeddings, your index, or your model. They just feed the retriever better questions.
What I Still Haven’t Figured Out
The entity anchor works well for explicit nouns — “the PR,” “that bug,” “Project Apollo.” It fails on abstract references: “the approach we discussed,” “that architecture.” Those need semantic matching, not entity lookup. I’m experimenting with embedding the conversation summary and doing a similarity search against a small index of “discussion topics,” but it’s early.
There’s also the multi-user problem. In a shared Slack channel or support thread, “we discussed” might mean someone else discussed it. The rewrite prompt needs to know who’s asking. I haven’t found a clean way to thread context per user without exploding storage.
Another open question: evaluation. How do you measure rewrite quality systematically? I’ve built a small eval set of conversation-retrieval pairs, but it’s labor-intensive to maintain. There’s no standard benchmark for “conversational retrieval quality” the way there is for standalone QA. If you know one, I’d love to hear about it.
If you’re shipping RAG to production, start with the conversation rewriter. It’s 50 lines of code, one extra LLM call, and it solves the most common failure mode I’ve seen across six different deployments. The rest is iterative.
What context-loss pattern has bitten you in production?
